105:4.Historical Error: Attributing the defeat of an army to miraculous stones of baked clay rather than the historical reality of a devastating plague demonstrates a historical error, adopting a mythical explanation for a natural event.

108:3.Theological Defect: Responding to Meccan pagans who mocked Muhammad for having no surviving sons, Allah reveals an eternal verse to fire back a localized, vindictive insult, calling the hater 'cut off' (al-abtar). As confirmed by Tafsir attributing this to Al-'As bin Wa'il, this portrays a deity embroiled in temporal, petty disputes rather than revealing transcendent truth.

111:1.Theological Defect: Instead of revealing transcendent truths, Allah devotes this supposedly eternal verse to hurl a petty, vindictive curse at Muhammad's uncle Abu Lahab ('Perish the hands of Abu Lahab'). The Tafsir explicitly contextualizes this as a divine retaliation for Abu Lahab's opposition, reducing divine revelation to a tool for settling Muhammad's familial disputes.

111:2.Theological Defect: Continuing the localized curse, this verse focuses on Abu Lahab's wealth failing him ('ma aghna anhu maluhu'). This portrays a vindictive Allah who uses His uncreated word to gloat over the temporal downfall of Muhammad's personal adversary.

111:3.Theological Defect: Allah specifically condemns Abu Lahab to a flaming fire ('sayasla naran dhata lahab'), illustrating a vindictive deity who eternalizes a localized, 7th-century family quarrel into the timeless Quranic text.

111:4.Theological Defect: The eternal word of God is used here to mockingly describe Abu Lahab's wife, Umm Jamil, as a 'carrier of firewood' (hammalata al-hatab). As confirmed by Tafsir, this vindictive and highly personalized attack contradicts the nature of a holy God, reducing His revelation to petty insults against a specific woman.

111:5.Theological Defect: This verse concludes the petty curse by describing a rope of palm fiber (masad) around the neck of Abu Lahab's wife. A God who uses eternal revelation to hurl such localized, vindictive insults against an individual woman demonstrates a severe theological defect, portraying God as petty and retaliatory.

112:3.Contradicts the Bible: By explicitly declaring that Allah 'has not begotten and was not begotten' (lam yalid walam yulad), this verse directly contradicts the core of the biblical Gospel, which testifies that Jesus is the eternally begotten Son of God (John 3:16). As the Tafsir confirms this denies God having any child or spouse, the Quran formally rejects the Triune nature of God and the historic Father-Son relationship.

113:2.Theological Defect: By instructing believers to seek refuge from 'the evil of what He has created' (min sharri ma khalaq), this verse portrays Allah as the active author and creator of evil. As confirmed by Tafsir stating this includes 'Hell, Iblis and his progeny,' this theological defect explicitly contradicts the biblical view of God as perfectly good and untainted by evil (1 John 1:5).
